sections.content:

<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check white corridor when player at cols 32-36

for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] > 10:
        print(f"White: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'R':
        print(f"Red: pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

- [[14, 32], [14, 38], [38, 38], [38, 26], [37, 26], [36, 25], [36, 20], [26, 20], [26, 31], [25, 32]]
This is a clockwise boundary. Let me trace it:
- Start at (14, 32), go right to (14, 38), down to (38, 38), left to (38, 26), up to (37, 26), left to (36, 25), left to (36, 20), up to (26, 20), right to (26, 31), up to (25, 32), back to (14, 32).
So when the player is at position 4, the white corridor's bottom part extends to rows 36-38, cols 26-38, and rows 26-36, cols 20-25.
This is different from the original white corridor's boundary, which extends to rows 38, cols 20-38.
So when the player is at position 4, the white corridor's bottom part is more restricted. The player can't reach rows 38, cols 20-25.

<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at rows 8-12, cols 32-36. Move DOWN to rows 14-18, cols 32-36, then RIGHT to cols 38-42, then DOWN.

for n in seg['nodes']:

        print(f"Player: {n['boundary']}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
